description:
You are a support engineer who has just received a request to collect a quick diagnostic
    snapshot from a customer’s system and place it on a shared “remote” drop-zone so that the
    back-line team can pick it up.  
    
    1. Source data that MUST be included  
       • /home/user/app/logs/app.log  
       • /home/user/app/config/settings.conf  
    
    2. What you must deliver  
       a. Create a compressed archive called “diag_bundle.tar.gz” that contains BOTH of the
          above files, keeping the directory structure inside the archive exactly as it is on
          disk (i.e. “app/logs/app.log” and “app/config/settings.conf”).  
       b. Place that archive inside the local workspace directory
          /home/user/diagnostics/ (create the directory if it is missing).  
       c. Synchronise (rsync) the archive so that an identical copy ends up in the
          drop-zone directory /home/user/remote-drop/.  Treat that directory as the
          “remote” location.  Preserve timestamps and permissions during transfer.  
    
    3. Verification artefact  
       After the rsync completes, generate a text file named /home/user/sync_status.log
       with EXACTLY the following two lines and nothing else (line-break = UNIX “\n”):  
       
           bundle_name:diag_bundle.tar.gz
           rsync_exit_code:0
       
       • “bundle_name:” MUST be followed by the exact file name you created.  
       • “rsync_exit_code:” MUST be the numeric exit code returned by rsync
         (success = 0).  
       • No extra spaces before or after the colon.  
    
    You should need no more than two or three terminal commands to:  
       • create the archive,  
       • run rsync,  
       • capture the exit code and write the status log.  
    
    The automated checker will verify:  
       • The presence of /home/user/diagnostics/diag_bundle.tar.gz.  
       • That the archive, when extracted, contains the two required files with the correct
         relative paths.  
       • That /home/user/remote-drop/ contains an identical copy of the archive.  
       • That /home/user/sync_status.log exists and its contents match the exact two-line
         format specified above.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing test fixtures -->
    Directory /home/user/app/logs/ exists with file:
        /home/user/app/logs/app.log
        Content (exact): "Log Start\n"  (final newline present)
        File mode: 0644

    Directory /home/user/app/config/ exists with file:
        /home/user/app/config/settings.conf
        Content (exact):
            "setting1=value1\nsetting2=value2\n"
        File mode: 0644

    Directory /home/user/remote-drop/ already exists and is writable by the
    unprivileged user (mode 0755).  It is initially empty.

    Expected results after the agent finishes:

    1. /home/user/diagnostics/diag_bundle.tar.gz exists.
       The tarball, when extracted, yields the following entries
       (mode 0644 for files, 0755 for intermediate directories):
           ./app/
           ./app/logs/
           ./app/logs/app.log          (content exactly "Log Start\n")
           ./app/config/
           ./app/config/settings.conf  (content exactly "setting1=value1\nsetting2=value2\n")

    2. /home/user/remote-drop/diag_bundle.tar.gz exists and is byte-for-byte
       identical to /home/user/diagnostics/diag_bundle.tar.gz.

    3. /home/user/sync_status.log exists with exact content (two lines, LF-terminated):
           bundle_name:diag_bundle.tar.gz\n
           rsync_exit_code:0\n

    The automated harness will untar the archive, compare file contents, and read the
    status log to confirm correctness.
